title: Sparse matrices in sparse analysis
speaker: Anna Gilbert
affiliation: University of Michigan; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: In this talk, I will give two vignettes on the theme of sparse matrices in sparse analysis. The first vignette covers work from compressive sensing in which we want to design sparse matrices (i.e., matrices with few non-zero entries) that we use to (linearly) sense or measure compressible signals. We also design algorithms such that, from these measurements and these matrices, we can efficiently recover a compressed, or sparse, representation of the sensed data.

proof_stages.label: Sparse recovery design problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk first formulates compressive sensing as the design of a short, preferably sparse measurement matrix A and a fast algorithm that recovers a roughly k-sparse signal from Ax with specified Lp/Lq approximation guarantees, randomness assumptions, recovery time, and noise robustness.

proof_stages.label: Application-driven method dichotomy
proof_stages.summary: Applications motivate why sparse structured matrices and fast update/recovery matter, after which the speaker contrasts dense geometric convex-optimization methods with sparse combinatorial greedy methods and asks what common principle makes both work.

proof_stages.label: Expander/RIP1 unification and decoding
proof_stages.summary: Sparse combinatorial measurement matrices are identified as bipartite expander adjacency matrices, and their expansion is shown to be equivalent to an L1 restricted isometry property that supports L1 minimization/null-space recovery guarantees and distinguishes them from RIP2 matrices.

proof_stages.label: Sparse metric repair formulation
proof_stages.summary: The second vignette models triangle inequalities as a sparse linear system on the vectorized distance matrix and asks for a sparse perturbation repairing a corrupted metric, arguing that L2 nearest-metric projection and naive L1 relaxation do not capture this L0 objective.

proof_stages.label: Cubic barrier and signed repair regimes
proof_stages.summary: Using the equivalence between metric checking and all-pairs shortest paths as a warning against truly subcubic detection, the problem is separated into decrease-only, increase-only, and unrestricted repair regimes whose sparse solutions behave differently.

proof_stages.label: Decrease-only repair via shortest paths
proof_stages.summary: For decrease-only repair, running Floyd–Warshall on the complete weighted graph gives the shortest-path metric, and the resulting perturbation is simultaneously sparsest and optimal in every Lp norm.

proof_stages.label: Increase-only and graph-metric hardness
proof_stages.summary: The increase-only repair problem is explained as combinatorially hard and NP-hard/NP-complete, then extended to graph metric repair with broken cycles, approximation algorithms parameterized by graph quantities, and data-imputation motivations.
